moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Polish Opening, an unusual first move that immediately signals we're not interested in standard theory. This move controls the c5 and a5 squares while preparing to fianchetto our light-squared bishop on b2, where it will exert long-term pressure on the g7-diagonal. The Polish Opening is tactically sharp and gains space on the queenside, but it requires precise play and knowledge of its specific ideas—White sacrifices some central control for active piece play and unexpected tactical resources. You'll find this opening most effective when you understand that it's not about dominating the center immediately, but about creating immediate threats that demand Black's attention.

Black develops actively and immediately challenges our intentions on the queenside by controlling d4, e5, and d8. This is the Grigorian Variation, where Black places the knight on its ideal square rather than retreating or playing a setup like ...e6 or ...d6. By developing with tempo and eyeing central squares, Black avoids passive positions and keeps counterplay alive. We should recognize that Black is not afraid of the unusual opening and has responded with solid development, so we'll need to follow up with our own constructive moves to maintain the initiative.
